description:
You are beginning a local reconnaissance phase for a penetration-testing engagement and need a quick snapshot of every TCP socket that is currently listening on the machine.

Objective:
1. Create a new directory  
   /home/user/pt_scan  
   if (and only if) it does not already exist.
2. Run one single terminal command that captures a list of every listening TCP socket and writes the raw console output, unmodified, to a file located at  
   /home/user/pt_scan/tcp_listen.log

Mandatory output specification (what the automated grader will look for):
• The file **/home/user/pt_scan/tcp_listen.log** must exist when you finish.  
• It must be plain ASCII text.  
• The first line of the file must start with the exact word “State” (capital S, lower-case rest).  
  This is the header line produced by the `ss` utility and assures that you captured the full command output, not merely filtered data.  
• The file must contain at least that header line; if there are no listening sockets, the header line alone is fine.

Constraints:
• Fulfil both steps using just **one** compound shell command (you may use operators such as “&&”, “;”, or pipes as needed).  
• Do **not** manually create or edit the log file with a text editor; the command itself must generate the log.  
• Do not remove any columns or transform the command’s native layout.

If executed correctly, an automated test will open /home/user/pt_scan/tcp_listen.log and confirm that its very first word is “State”. It will also verify that the file is inside /home/user/pt_scan.
truth:
The judge will verify:
1. The directory /home/user/pt_scan exists (type: directory, permissions 755).
2. The file /home/user/pt_scan/tcp_listen.log exists (type: regular file, permissions 644, size ≥ 6 bytes).
3. The file’s very first five characters are the ASCII sequence: 53 74 61 74 65 (“State”).
4. (No further content requirements—header alone is acceptable.)
